---
title: "Git for people who've been meaning to learn Git (but haven't yet)"
subtitle: "NIHR Maudsley BRC<br>23 April 2026"
author: "Dr Ewan Carr"
institute: "Department of Biostatistics & Health Informatics<br>King's College London"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.1
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    center: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    include-after-body:
      text: |
        <script>
        document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function () {
          var t = document.querySelector('#title-slide h1.title');
          if (t) {
            t.innerHTML = t.innerHTML.replace(
              ' (but haven\u2019t yet)',
              '<br><span style="display:inline-block">(but haven\u2019t yet)</span>'
            );
          }
        });
        </script>
---

## Goals for today

1. **A clear mental model of Git** — what it is, what a repository is, and how GitHub fits in.
2. **Your first repository** — how to start tracking a project without the workflow feeling mysterious.
3. **A real commit pushed to GitHub** — enough confidence to try this on your own work.

[14:00–15:00 Concepts and demonstrations]{.muted}<br>
[15:00–16:00 Practice]{.muted}

---

::: {.center style="text-align:center; margin: 0 auto; max-width: 70%;"}
![](images/ballot-box.png){width="70%" fig-align="center"}

[Your answers to the survey...]{.muted}
:::

---

## What is Git? {.sec background-color="#28A7A7"}

---

::: {.center style="font-size: 2.2em; line-height: 1.6; text-align: center; max-width: 80%; margin: 0 auto;"}
Git is ***software*** that ***tracks changes*** to your files.
:::

---

## Without version control

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="70%"}

- File names become versions: `analysis_final_v4_Ewan.R`, `THIS_ONE_WORKS.R`
- No record of what changed or why
- Collaboration (e.g., via email) creates diverging copies with no easy way to merge them
:::
::: {.column width="30%"}
![](images/not-fine.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::::

---

::: {style="font-size: 1.1em;"}

**Git**
: [Software]{.alert} on your computer that tracks changes to files and records project history.

**Repository**
: A [project folder]{.alert} that Git is watching, including the hidden `.git` folder that stores the history.

**GitHub**
: A [website]{.alert} that hosts repositories online for backup, sharing, and collaboration.

:::

You can use Git entirely locally, without GitHub.

---

## How you interact with Git

1. Through the [command line]{.alert}:

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em;"}
```bash
git init
git add cleaning.R
git commit -m "Add initial cleaning script"
```
:::

2. Through a [graphical client]{.alert} (e.g., GitHub Desktop, Tower).

[Different interfaces, but doing the same thing underneath.]{.muted}

---

## Local vs. remote repositories

Local repository
: The working copy [on your computer]{.alert}. You can edit files and make commits offline.

Remote repository
: A linked copy hosted [online]{.alert} (e.g., GitHub), containing the complete history of your repository.

We can send and receive changes from the remote. This is referred to as "pushing" and "pulling".

---

![](images/flow-1.png)

---

![](images/flow-2.png)

---

## Why Git?

:::: {.columns style="font-size:0.8em;"}

::: {.column width="33%"}
[1. Complete history]{.reason-card-title}

- Every change annotated with a message
- Go back to any earlier version
- Compare exactly what changed between two points
- Remember why you made decisions months later
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
[2. Collaboration]{.reason-card-title}

- One repository, multiple contributors
- Everyone works locally, shares changes via the repository
- Git records who changed what, and when
- Pull, edit, commit, push
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
[3. Open research]{.reason-card-title}

- Sits alongside a paper or report
- Readers can inspect the files, `README`, and history
- Start private, make public when ready
- Funders and journals increasingly expect this
:::

::::

---

![](images/pmsims-repository.png){width="92%" fig-align="center"}

---

## How it works {.sec background-color="#6B4E9B"}

---

## Working with a local repository

:::: {.key-terms-grid style="grid-template-columns: repeat(3, minmax(0, 1fr));"}

::: {.key-term-card .key-term-file-state}
::: {.key-term-title}
Tracked vs. untracked
:::
New files in your folder are invisible to Git until you add them. Once added, Git watches them for changes.
:::

::: {.key-term-card .key-term-staging}
::: {.key-term-title}
Staging
:::
Before committing, you choose which changes to include. This lets you be deliberate about what goes into each commit.
:::

::: {.key-term-card .key-term-commit}
::: {.key-term-title}
Commit
:::
A saved snapshot of your project at a point in time, with a message, author, and timestamp.
:::

::::

---

## The workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
<div class="workflow-steps">
  <div class="workflow-step active"><span class="workflow-step-number">1</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Initialise repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">2</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Add files to the repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">3</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose first commit contents</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">4</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Make first commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">5</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Edit a file</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">6</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose which changes to commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">7</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Commit again</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step workflow-step-repeat"><span class="workflow-step-number">...</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Repeat</span></div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
<div class="workflow-definition">
  <div class="workflow-definition-kicker">Step 1</div>
  <div class="workflow-definition-title">Start the repository</div>
  <p>Git creates the hidden <code>.git</code> folder inside your project. Your files stay where they are.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

---

## The workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
<div class="workflow-steps">
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">1</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Initialise repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step active"><span class="workflow-step-number">2</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Add files to the repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">3</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose first commit contents</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">4</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Make first commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">5</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Edit a file</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">6</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose which changes to commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">7</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Commit again</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step workflow-step-repeat"><span class="workflow-step-number">...</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Repeat</span></div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
<div class="workflow-definition">
  <div class="workflow-definition-kicker">Step 2</div>
  <div class="workflow-definition-title">Add files to the folder and tell Git to track them</div>
  <p>Copy your project files in, then tell Git which ones to watch.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

---

## The workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
<div class="workflow-steps">
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">1</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Initialise repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">2</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Add files to the repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step active"><span class="workflow-step-number">3</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose first commit contents</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">4</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Make first commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">5</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Edit a file</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">6</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose which changes to commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">7</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Commit again</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step workflow-step-repeat"><span class="workflow-step-number">...</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Repeat</span></div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
<div class="workflow-definition">
  <div class="workflow-definition-kicker">Step 3</div>
  <div class="workflow-definition-title">Choose what goes into the first commit</div>
  <p>Git calls this staging. You are choosing which tracked files should go into the next commit.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

---

## The workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
<div class="workflow-steps">
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">1</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Initialise repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">2</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Add files to the repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">3</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose first commit contents</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step active"><span class="workflow-step-number">4</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Make first commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">5</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Edit a file</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">6</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose which changes to commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">7</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Commit again</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step workflow-step-repeat"><span class="workflow-step-number">...</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Repeat</span></div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
<div class="workflow-definition">
  <div class="workflow-definition-kicker">Step 4</div>
  <div class="workflow-definition-title">Save the first version</div>
  <p>Your first commit gives the repository a starting point and a message explaining what it contains.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

---

## The workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
<div class="workflow-steps">
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">1</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Initialise repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">2</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Add files to the repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">3</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose first commit contents</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">4</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Make first commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step active"><span class="workflow-step-number">5</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Edit a file</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">6</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose which changes to commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">7</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Commit again</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step workflow-step-repeat"><span class="workflow-step-number">...</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Repeat</span></div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
<div class="workflow-definition">
  <div class="workflow-definition-kicker">Step 5</div>
  <div class="workflow-definition-title">Work normally</div>
  <p>Edit a tracked file and save it on your computer. Git notices the change and waits for you to decide what to do next.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

---

## The workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
<div class="workflow-steps">
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">1</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Initialise repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">2</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Add files to the repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">3</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose first commit contents</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">4</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Make first commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">5</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Edit a file</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step active"><span class="workflow-step-number">6</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose which changes to commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">7</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Commit again</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step workflow-step-repeat"><span class="workflow-step-number">...</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Repeat</span></div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
<div class="workflow-definition">
  <div class="workflow-definition-kicker">Step 6</div>
  <div class="workflow-definition-title">Select what belongs in the next version</div>
  <p>This is staging again. Choose which edits should go into the next commit.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

---

## The workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
<div class="workflow-steps">
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">1</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Initialise repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">2</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Add files to the repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">3</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose first commit contents</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">4</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Make first commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">5</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Edit a file</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">6</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose which changes to commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step active"><span class="workflow-step-number">7</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Commit again</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step workflow-step-repeat"><span class="workflow-step-number">...</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Repeat</span></div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
<div class="workflow-definition">
  <div class="workflow-definition-kicker">Step 7</div>
  <div class="workflow-definition-title">Save the next version and repeat</div>
  <p>Make another commit with a clear message. From that point on, the pattern is edit, choose, commit, repeat.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

---

## The workflow

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
<div class="workflow-steps">
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">1</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Initialise repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">2</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Add files to the repository</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">3</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose first commit contents</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">4</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Make first commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">5</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Edit a file</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">6</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Choose which changes to commit</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step"><span class="workflow-step-number">7</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Commit again</span></div>
  <div class="workflow-step-gap"></div>
  <div class="workflow-step active workflow-step-repeat"><span class="workflow-step-number">...</span><span class="workflow-step-label">Repeat</span></div>
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}
<div class="workflow-definition">
  <div class="workflow-definition-kicker">Step 7</div>
  <div class="workflow-definition-title">Save the next version and repeat</div>
  <p>Continue working, and repeat the process as needed.</p>
</div>
:::
::::

---

A commit gives you a saved version of your staged changes, together with its message, author, and timestamp.

::: {.center style="text-align:center; margin: 0 auto; max-width: 80%; padding: 0px;"}
![](images/annotated-history.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}
:::

- Browse the timeline of saved versions.
- Compare exactly what changed between two points.
- Recover an earlier version safely if something goes wrong.

---

## <span class="sec-kicker">DEMONSTRATION</span><span class="sec-title">Seeing the workflow in action</span> {.sec .demo-sec background-color="#1E7F71"}

---

We'll work with a downloaded `example/` project folder, which includes:

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column style="width:55%; flex:0 0 55%;"}
`README.md`
: A short description of the project

`scripts/clean.R`
: The cleaning script

`data/admissions.csv`
: The raw data — present locally, but we'll tell Git to ignore it

`outputs/model_predictions.csv`
: Generated outputs — also ignored
:::

::: {.column style="width:45%; flex:0 0 45%;"}
::: {.box}
Our goal is to:

1. Create a new repository in GitHub Desktop
2. Copy the project files in and ignore `data/`
3. Make two commits.
:::
:::

::::

---

## Demo 1 · Initialise and first commit

::: {.task-panel}
<div class="task-item">**File → New Repository** → name it `admissions-example` → **Create Repository**</div>
<div class="task-item">Copy the contents of `example/` into the new `admissions-example` folder</div>
<div class="task-item">Return to GitHub Desktop — the files appear in the Changes panel</div>
<div class="task-item">Right-click `data` → **Ignore all files in `data/`**; repeat for `outputs` — GitHub Desktop writes `.gitignore` automatically</div>
<div class="task-item">Review the remaining files: `README.md`, `scripts/`, `.gitignore`</div>
<div class="task-item">Write commit message: *Initial commit: add admissions example project*</div>
<div class="task-item">Click **Commit to main**</div>
:::

---

## Demo 1 · Make a change and commit again

::: {.task-panel}
<div class="task-item">Open `scripts/clean.R`, add a comment at the top, save</div>
<div class="task-item">GitHub Desktop shows the change in the Changes panel</div>
<div class="task-item">Click the file to show the **diff**</div>
<div class="task-item">Write commit message: *Add comment to cleaning script*</div>
<div class="task-item">Click **Commit to main**</div>
:::

---

## Demo 1 · View the history

::: {.task-panel}
<div class="task-item">Click the **History** tab</div>
<div class="task-item">Two commits are listed, with message, author, and timestamp</div>
<div class="task-item">Click each commit to see exactly what changed</div>
:::

---

## How to do this on the command line?

Initialise, add, and commit:

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em;"}
```bash
git init                        # Initialise the repository
git add README.md scripts/      # Stage the files to track
git commit -m "Initial commit"  # Save the first snapshot
```
:::

Make a change to `scripts/clean.R`, then:

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em;"}
```bash
git status                      # See what's changed
git add scripts/clean.R         # Stage the changed file
git commit -m "Add comment to cleaning script"
git log --oneline               # View the history
```
:::

---

## What if you already have a project folder?

Use `git init` to initialise the repository, then open it in GitHub Desktop:

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em;"}
```bash
git init
```
:::

Then in GitHub Desktop: **File → Add Local Repository** → choose the folder.

From there, review the Changes panel, ignore anything that shouldn't be tracked, and make your first commit — exactly as we just did.

::: {.callout-warning}
If you use **File → New Repository** inside a folder that already contains files, GitHub Desktop will stage everything automatically. That is usually not what you want.
:::

---

## <span class="sec-kicker">DEMONSTRATION</span><span class="sec-title">Going online</span> {.sec .demo-sec background-color="#C24B40"}

---

## Going online

- The repository we just created only lives on our computer.

- We can work entirely offline, committing changes and building up a history using Git.

- However, to share it with others, we need to connect it to a **remote** repository.

- We’re using GitHub, but there are several online hosting platforms for Git repositories (e.g. GitLab, Bitbucket).

---

The remote repository is a linked copy of your local repository that is hosted online (e.g., GitHub).

![](images/local-remote.png){fig-align="center"}

Going online adds a shared copy of the repository. It does not replace the local one on your computer.

---

## Connect, then push

1. Connect your local repository to the GitHub repository that will hold the online copy.
2. Push your existing commits so GitHub receives that history and creates the remote copy.
3. After that, future pushes update the same remote repository, and pulls bring those shared changes back to your computer.

::: {.callout-tip}
GitHub Desktop wraps this into a simple publish flow. The command line shows the same ideas as separate steps.
:::

---

## Signing in to GitHub

### GitHub Desktop

*Windows*

<kbd>File</kbd> → <kbd>Options</kbd> → <kbd>Accounts</kbd>

*Mac*

<kbd>GitHub Desktop</kbd> → <kbd>Settings</kbd> → <kbd>Accounts</kbd>

### Command line

* Use the GitHub command line client:
  `gh auth login`

* Or follow GitHub's [SSH setup guide][ssh-setup]

[Full instructions are on the workshop setup page.]{.muted}

[ssh-setup]: https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/generating-a-new-ssh-key-and-adding-it-to-the-ssh-agent

---

## Demo 2 · Create a repository on GitHub and connect it

::: {.task-panel}
<div class="task-item">Go to github.com → **+** → **New repository**</div>
<div class="task-item">Name it `admissions-example`, set it to **Private**</div>
<div class="task-item">Leave README, `.gitignore`, and licence unticked</div>
<div class="task-item">Click **Create repository**</div>
<div class="task-item">In GitHub Desktop, click **Publish repository** → confirm name → **Publish**</div>
:::

---

## Demo 2 · One more commit → push → watch it arrive

::: {.task-panel}
<div class="task-item">Make a small edit to `README.md` locally and save</div>
<div class="task-item">Commit the change</div>
<div class="task-item">Click **Push origin**</div>
<div class="task-item">Refresh GitHub and watch the new commit appear</div>
:::

---

## 🧠 Mini quiz {.sec background-color="#EA526F"}

---

![](images/mentimeter_qr_code.png){width="30%" fig-align="center"}

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: 0.5em;">
<a href="https://www.menti.com/alrgybisqx9m"><code>menti.com/alrgybisqx9m</code></a>
</div>

---

## Best practices {.sec background-color="#28A7A7"}

---

## What should go in a commit?

* A commit should represent **one coherent change**.
* Think "what did I just do?", not "which file did I edit?"
* A single commit will often include changes across multiple files

*If you can describe the change in one sentence, it’s probably a good commit.*

## Writing good commit messages

A commit message should explain what changed **and why**.

::: {style="font-size: 1.5em; padding: 0.1em;"}
```
Short summary line describing what changed

An optional longer description, explaining why it
changed and adding any important details.
```
:::

The description should explain the problem being addressed, the approach taken,
and any assumptions or trade-offs involved.


---

:::: {.columns}


::: {.column .good-examples-column width="70%"}

### 🚀 Good examples

::: {.box color="green"}
**Add cross-validation to model pipeline**<br>
<br>

Implements 5-fold CV for logistic regression model. This replaces the previous
single train/test split to reduce variance in performance estimates.
:::

::: {.box color="green"}
**Check for missing values before model fitting**
<br><br>

Introduces a check to stop execution if key variables contain missing
values. This prevents silent failures and unexpected model behaviour.
:::

:::

::: {.column .bad-examples-column width="30%"}

### 🔥 Bad examples

- `Monday's work`
- `Changes`
- `Update`
- `Final version`

:::

::::

---

## You can (and should) tell Git to ignore some files

Create a file `.gitignore` in the project folder. Use this file to omit files
you don't want under version control. For example:

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em;"}
```bash
data/       # Ignore everything in 'data'
outputs/    # and 'outputs'

.Rhistory   # Ignore a specific file
.RData
*.Rproj     # Ignore any file ending in .Rproj
```
:::

* **Yes:** Scripts, code, READMEs, manuscript text, config files.
* **No:** Raw or derived data, large outputs, model files, credentials, API keys

---

::: {.center style="color: #8a001a; font-weight: 600; font-size: 2.2em; line-height: 1.6; text-align: center; max-width: 80%; margin: 0 auto;"}

Never commit patient data.
:::

---

Add any data folders (e.g., `data`) to your `.gitignore` file **before** any data files are added.

::: {.callout-warning}
**Once sensitive data is committed to a public repository, deleting the file later is not enough.**

Git history can still contain the earlier version, so pushing first and deleting later does not undo the exposure.
:::

---

### March 2026:

Researchers using UK Biobank data committed data folders locally, pushed them to GitHub, and then exposed them publicly.

![](images/guardian-breach.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}

---

## Branches {.sec background-color="#2D3561"}

---


A branch is a separate line of work within the same repository.

Until now, we’ve been thinking about a single history. This is usually called
the `main` branch.

![](images/branching/single-branch.png){width="82%" fig-align="center"}

---

Git lets us have multiple branches:

![](images/branching/two-branches.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## How? First, create and switch to a new branch

On the command line, use `git switch -c` to create a new branch and move to it
immediately. Here, I've named the branch `add-figure-2`:

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em;"}
```bash
git switch -c add-figure-2
```
:::

![](images/branching/branching-flow-1.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}

---

On GitHub Desktop, choose <kbd>Branch</kbd>→<kbd>New Branch...</kbd>

![](images/new-branch-github-desktop.png){fig-align="center"}

Note that new commits now go on that branch, not `main`.

---

## When you switch branches, your files switch too

![](images/branching/branching-flow-2.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}

- Git updates your working directory to match the branch you switched to.
- Your work on the other branch is still there in its commits.
- To go back, switch back:

::: {style="font-size: 1.6em;"}
```bash
git switch main
```
:::

---

## When you're done, delete or merge back

::: {.center}
![](images/branching/branching-flow-3.png){width="70%" fig-align="center"}
:::

### Branches are a safe place to experiment

* If it works, you can **merge** it back into the main branch. Merging means
  bringing the commits from that branch into the main line of the project

* If not, you **delete** the branch and continue on main.

---

## Demonstration · Switch to a branch and back again

::: {.task-panel}
<div class="task-item">Start on <strong>main</strong></div>
<div class="task-item">Create and switch: <code>git switch -c add-readme-note</code></div>
<div class="task-item">Edit <code>README.md</code>, save, and commit the change</div>
<div class="task-item">Switch back: <code>git switch main</code></div>
<div class="task-item">The working directory returns to the <strong>main</strong> version</div>
<div class="task-item">Switch again: <code>git switch add-readme-note</code></div>
<div class="task-item">The committed edit reappears</div>
:::

---

## Collaboration {.sec background-color="#6B4E9B"}

---

![](images/collab-1.png){fig-align="center"}

---

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
Everyone has a local copy. Push and pull to the remote.
:::

![](images/collab-2.png){fig-align="center"}

---

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
Everyone has a local copy. Push and pull to the remote.
:::

![](images/collab-3.png){fig-align="center"}

---

## Use feature branches when collaborating

Never work directly on `main`. Create a **feature branch** for each piece of
work.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Example branch names

- `cleaning-fix-missing-values`
- `add-figure-2`
- `update-inclusion-criteria`
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Why?

- Keeps `main` stable
- Lets you work safely without breaking anything
- Makes it easier to review changes
:::

::::

---

## Pull requests (PR)

A pull request is a way of asking to [merge a branch]{.alert} into `main`.

1. Create a new branch (e.g. `implement-xgboost-model`)
2. Make and commit changes on that branch
3. On GitHub, open a pull request to merge into `main`

The PR allows others to [review and approve]{.alert} your code, or suggest changes.

---

## A simple workflow to follow

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column style="width:48%; flex:0 0 48%;"}
**When you start work**

1. Pull the latest version of `main`
2. Create a new feature branch

**While working**

3. Make small commits with clear messages
:::

::: {.column style="width:52%; flex:0 0 52%;"}
**When finished**

4. Push your branch to GitHub
5. Open a Pull Request
6. Get it reviewed and merged
:::

::::

---

## Forks: your own copy of a repository

A **fork** is your own copy of someone else's repository — useful when you don't have write access to the original.

::: {style="border-left: 4px solid rgba(45,53,97,0.25); background: rgba(45,53,97,0.04); padding: 0.5em 0.8em 0.5em 1em; margin: 0.5em 0;"}
1. Fork the repository on GitHub
2. Clone your fork from GitHub to your local machine
3. Make your changes, commit, and push back to your fork
4. Open a pull request to the original repository
:::

We'll try this in the collaborative exercise.

---

![](images/fork-demo-1.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}

---

![](images/fork-demo-2.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}

---

## Where to go next

[Happy Git with R](https://happygitwithr.com)
: Git, GitHub, and RStudio — the best starting point for R users.

[Pro Git](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2)
: The definitive Git reference, free online.

[Software Carpentry: Version Control with Git](https://swcarpentry.github.io/git-novice/)
: A self-paced hands-on lesson for researchers.

[Oh Shit, Git!?](https://ohshitgit.com)
: Plain-English fixes for common mistakes.

[Excuse me, do you have a moment to talk about version control?](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00031305.2017.1399928)
: Bryan (2018) — the academic case for version control in research.

---

::: {.center style="font-size: 2.4em; font-weight: 600; text-align: center;"}
5-minute break
:::

---

## Exercises

::: {style="font-size:1.3em;"}
0. Check your setup
1. Your first local repository
2. Push to GitHub
3. Make a change and push again
4. Add your name to a shared repository
:::

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: 0.5em; font-size: 1.8em;">
<a href="https://tinyurl.com/brc-git-github"><code>tinyurl.com/brc-git-github</code></a>
</div>


---

## Appendix: First-time setup {.sec background-color="#C24B40"}

---

## `git config` — three lines to run once

```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
```

`user.name` / `user.email`
: Your identity on every commit

`init.defaultBranch`
: Optional hygiene: use `main` instead of the old `master` default when you create new repositories from the command line

GitHub Desktop usually sets the important parts for you. These commands are mainly for command-line users who want Git configured explicitly.

---

## Where does `.gitconfig` live?

| Platform | Location | How to open |
|---|---|---|
| **Mac** | `~/.gitconfig` | `open ~/.gitconfig` or any text editor |
| **Windows** | `C:\Users\YourName\.gitconfig` | Notepad, VS Code, or `notepad $HOME\.gitconfig` in PowerShell |

::: {.callout-note}
`.gitconfig` is a hidden file. To show it:
**Mac** — `Cmd+Shift+.` in Finder
**Windows** — View → Show → Hidden items
:::

You can edit it directly in a text editor, or use `git config --global` commands. Both do the same thing.
